questions.question: Differentiate between user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Mention at least two advantages and two disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: User-level threads are managed by user-level libraries, kernel is unaware
Kernel-level threads are managed by OS kernel directly
Advantages of ULT: fast context switching, flexible scheduling, portable
Disadvantages of ULT: cannot utilize multiprocessors, blocking I/O affects all threads
Advantages of KLT: true parallelism, kernel can schedule on multiple cores
Disadvantages of KLT: slower context switching, kernel overhead

questions.question: Explain the concept of a race condition. How can it be prevented? Give two synchronization mechanisms with examples.
keyPoints: Race condition: outcome depends on timing of concurrent threads
Occurs when multiple threads access shared data simultaneously
Prevention: mutual exclusion using synchronization primitives
Mutex: lock/unlock to protect critical section
Semaphore: signal/wait for resource access
Monitor: high-level synchronization construct

questions.question: Explain the three multithreading models: many-to-one, one-to-one, and many-to-many. Give one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Many-to-one: multiple user threads to one kernel thread - efficient but no parallelism
One-to-one: one user thread per kernel thread - parallelism but scalability issues
Many-to-many: many user threads to many kernel threads - best of both worlds
Two-level model: combination of one-to-one and many-to-many

questions.question: What is Amdahl's Law? A program takes 50 seconds to run on a single processor. If 20% of the program is sequential, calculate the maximum speedup achievable with 4 processors.
keyPoints: Amdahl's Law: speedup limited by sequential portion of program
Formula: Speedup = 1/(S + (1-S)/N)
S = sequential fraction = 0.2
N = number of processors = 4

questions.question: Explain the thread lifecycle with appropriate state diagram. What are the possible transitions between states?
keyPoints: Thread states: New, Ready, Running, Blocked/Waiting, Terminated
New: thread created but not started
Ready: waiting for CPU allocation
Running: executing on CPU
Blocked: waiting for I/O or event
Terminated: completed execution
Transitions: New→Ready, Ready→Running, Running→Ready, Running→Blocked, Blocked→Ready, Running→Terminated

questions.question: What is a deadlock? Discuss the four necessary conditions for deadlock occurrence. How can deadlocks be prevented?
keyPoints: Deadlock: threads waiting indefinitely for resources held by others
Mutual Exclusion: only one thread can use resource at a time
Hold and Wait: threads hold resources while waiting for others
No Preemption: resources cannot be forcibly taken
Circular Wait: circular chain of waiting threads
Prevention: eliminate any one condition - ordered resource allocation, preempt resources

questions.question: What is a thread pool? Explain its advantages and describe a scenario where it would be beneficial.
keyPoints: Thread pool: collection of pre-created worker threads
Advantages: reduces thread creation overhead, limits concurrent threads, better resource management
Applications: web servers, database connections, parallel processing tasks
Scenario: handling multiple client requests in web server - reuse threads instead of creating new ones for each request

questions.question: With the help of a Producer-Consumer problem, explain how semaphores can be used for thread synchronization. Write pseudocode for both producer and consumer processes using semaphores.
keyPoints: Producer-Consumer: bounded buffer problem
Three semaphores: mutex (mutual exclusion), empty (count of empty slots), full (count of filled slots)
Producer: wait(empty), wait(mutex), add item, signal(mutex), signal(full)
Consumer: wait(full), wait(mutex), remove item, signal(mutex), signal(empty)
Semaphores handle both synchronization and mutual exclusion
Solution prevents race conditions and ensures bounded buffer is not overflowed/underflowed

questions.question: Explain the Readers-Writers problem in thread synchronization. How can it be solved using read-write locks? Discuss the different variants of the problem.
keyPoints: Readers-Writers: multiple readers can access simultaneously, writers need exclusive access
First readers-writers: readers have priority - writers may starve
Second writers-first: writers have priority - readers may starve
Third fair: no starvation for either
Implementation: read-write lock with readcount variable
Readers: wait on read lock, increment readcount, signal, read data, decrement, signal if zero
Writers: wait for write lock, acquire exclusive access, write, release lock

questions.question: Discuss the challenges of multithreading on multicore systems. How does thread affinity and cache coherence affect performance?
keyPoints: Multicore: multiple CPU cores on single chip
Challenges: load balancing across cores, minimizing cache misses, thread affinity
Thread affinity: binding threads to specific cores
Benefits of affinity: better cache utilization, reduced migration overhead
Cache coherence: ensuring all cores see consistent data
False sharing: when threads modify different variables sharing same cache line
NUMA awareness: memory access latency varies based on core location
Amdahl's Law limits speedup based on sequential portion

questions.question: What is a monitor in concurrent programming? Explain how it solves the mutual exclusion and synchronization problems with an example.
keyPoints: Monitor: high-level synchronization construct
Encapsulates shared data and procedures that operate on that data
Provides mutual exclusion automatically - only one thread can execute inside monitor at a time
Condition variables for synchronization: wait, signal, broadcast
Example: bounded buffer as monitor
Producer: wait if buffer full, add item, signal waiting consumers
Consumer: wait if buffer empty, remove item, signal waiting producers
Advantages: easier to reason about, avoids explicit lock management
Languages supporting monitors: Java (synchronized), Pascal, Mesa
